text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic gastric bypass, beginning with the dissection of the angular incisure between the esophagus and proximal stomach. The lesser curvature is dissected, and an opening is made between the lesser curvature and the lesser omentum to access the lesser sac. A blue load stapler is used to construct a 5-6 cm gastric pouch off the lesser curvature, guided by an Evo tube.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and the adjacent inferior mesenteric vein. The small bowel is transected approximately 40 centimeters from the ligament using a white load stapler. On the right side of the screen, the biliopancreatic limb is visible, marked with a clip, while the left side shows the Roux limb. The Roux limb is brought up to the gastric pouch, and a two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis is performed using micro-stitches.

The surgeon ties the first stitch of the anastomosis and runs the first layer along the extension. An opening is made in the gastric pouch using a scalpel, approximately 1.5 centimeters in size. A similar but slightly smaller opening is created in the Roux limb. The second layer of the anastomosis is constructed using micro stitches, with careful attention to the corners, taking full thickness bites of both the small bowel and gastric wall.

The surgeon stops stitching just shy of the corner of the anastomosis and begins a new stitch on the right side. Approximately four separate stitches are used for this anastomosis. The EO-II tube is passed from the gastric pouch into the small bowel, ensuring the back wall of the anastomosis is not involved. The anterior wall of the anastomosis is completed, followed by the final layer using gaiter stitches with two micro stitches.

The surgeon tests the anastomosis by injecting air and submerging it under water to check for leaks. Subsequently, the surgeon runs the roux limb for approximately 100 to 150 centimeters to construct the jejunojejunostomy. Stay stitches are placed between the roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb, followed by the creation of enterotomies in each limb using a 60mm stapler with a white load.

The surgeon closes the resulting defect in two layers using ovacro stitches, followed by closing the mesenteric defect with sutures. The completed jugular jejunostomy is visible, and silk sutures are used to close a pituitous defect.
We normally conclude the bypass by doing an endoscopy and also retesting the anastrosis a second time. That's pretty simple. And that concludes the Dashboard Podcast.
